text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic appendectomy for perforated appendicitis, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg. The small bowel is adhered to the perforation site in the right pelvis, and pus under pressure is visible. Cultures are taken, and the area is irrigated.

My feeling for these cases is a surgical first approach. I don't usually sit with conservative therapy on patients with perforated appendicitis unless there is a really dominant abscess. Or in a patient who is not a surgical candidate, I tend to just go in and surgically deal with the disease. I find that in nearly all cases you can successfully complete the appendectomy without the need for ileocolic resection. And I'm comfortable dealing with these cases in this manner that you see here.

The surgeon carefully lifts a loop of ileum off the perforation site to locate the appendix. The base of the appendix is visible on the lateral aspect of the ileal mesentery, with the surgeon performing blunt dissection to free it. The tip of the appendix is grasped, revealing its position in the lateral aspect of the ileal mesentery.

The surgeon carefully frees the terminal ileum mesentery from the sigmoid colon and reflects the ileum to the upper abdomen, revealing the appendix on the lateral aspect of the ileal mesentery. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon elevates the appendix mesentery away from the ileal mesentery, ensuring precise dissection without encroaching on surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mesentery back to the cecum, preparing to staple. The cecum and the base of the appendix appear normal, allowing for safe stapling. The surgeon takes down some lateral attachments of the cecum to increase mobility.

The surgeon has freed the appendix from the pelvic sidewall, allowing for safer manipulation of the appendiceal mesentery. The mesentery is being carefully dissected back to the cecum, preparing for safe stapling.

The surgeon observes the hemostatic staple line and applies Surgicel to the ileal mesentery for additional hemostasis. The ileum and omentum are repositioned over the surgical site, with no drains left in place.
